meeting_purpose_blurb: Council examines particular topics in depth. No official decisions are made and no public comment is taken.
lead_headline: Council Examines Wildfire Plans and Utility Rate Framework in Double Workshop

type_specific.meeting_summary: The Arvada City Council held two workshops on May 12, 2026. In the first, city emergency management staff and Arvada Fire presented wildfire preparedness efforts including the Community Wildfire Protection Plan, evacuation strategies, public safety power shut-off responses, and mitigation projects. In the second, utility staff briefed council on the foundational framework for upcoming 2027 water, wastewater, and stormwater rate-setting decisions, including infrastructure planning, affordability programs, and a proposed community advisory group.
type_specific.lead_headline: Council Examines Wildfire Plans and Utility Rate Framework in Double Workshop
type_specific.meeting_purpose_blurb: Council examines particular topics in depth. No official decisions are made and no public comment is taken.

workshop_topics.title: Wildfire Mitigation and Response Strategies
workshop_topics.tagline: City and Arvada Fire outline preparedness, evacuation gaps, and mitigation tools
workshop_topics.lede: City emergency management staff and Arvada Fire representatives briefed council on the full scope of Arvada's wildfire strategy, including the recently completed Community Wildfire Protection Plan (CWPP), a new Wildfire Resiliency Code adopted by council in April, preparedness levels and staffing protocols, neighborhood outreach efforts, and responses to Xcel Energy's public safety power shut-offs (PSPS). Staff also identified a gap in formal policy tools for evaluating evacuation impacts in new development proposals and noted that funding for larger mitigation projects is being actively sought.

options.label: Continue and expand current multi-layered mitigation approach
options.summary: Maintain and build on existing strategies: CWPP implementation, Wildfire Resiliency Code enforcement, neighborhood outreach, home hardening assessments, evacuation zone mapping, fuels reduction projects, prescribed fire where feasible, and coordination with Jefferson County and regional partners.
options.endorsement: Presented by staff as the current operational approach; council members expressed broad support.

options.label: Add formal evacuation analysis requirement for development proposals
options.summary: Create a policy mechanism — modeled on Colorado Springs and Boulder County ordinances — that formally ties evacuation analysis into the development review process, rather than relying on informal staff collaboration.
options.endorsement: Raised by staff as a gap to address; no council member explicitly opposed it.

options.label: Expand pre-event PSPS community information and charging station network
options.summary: Develop and socialize in advance a clear formula for where charging stations and warming centers will be located during PSPS events, so residents can plan before an outage occurs rather than learning during the event.

quote.speaker: Bob Fifer
quote.text: I just curious how much we're engaged with them to address that since some of it's out of the city. Um, as just a side note, just so you know, and I think you know this, you know, Highway 72 to Highway 119 is the highest burn probability in the state. So, I'm interested in that.
quote.context_excerpt: Um, obviously they got to hire 45 people or whatever, but um, I just curious how much we're engaged with them to address that since some of it's out of the city. Um, as just a side note, just so you know, and I think you know this, you know, Highway 72 to Highway 119 is the highest burn probability in the state. So, I'm interested in that. Uh, if I put my day job on, I will tell you we started cutting down trees today on Highway 72 for 40 miles.

quote.speaker: Lauren Simpson
quote.text: I'm also thinking about what we can do in the immediate term to help our residents. Um, and this, I guess, maybe is a sort of a request for support. Are there information that we can be sharing out now about, you know, what to have in your go bag? I know we have that, but where will charging stations be set up? Where can the public just know in advance before uh an event happens?
quote.context_excerpt: However, I'm also thinking about what we can do in the immediate term to help our residents. Um, and this, I guess, maybe is a sort of a request for support. Are there information that we can be sharing out now about, you know, what to have in your go bag? I know we have that, but where will charging stations be set up? Where can the public just know in advance before uh an event happens? Okay, I'm going to have to work from home today, but I'm going to grab my laptop and I'm going to go here

quote.speaker: Randy Moorman
quote.text: I'm I'm going to sound like a broken record council but I think you know having a more sustainable local source of energy uh to provide our residents in those times of the shut off whether it's a micro grid system, you know, solar gardens that we can tap into in battery system. There's many things we can be looking at to help have a more robust, resilient community when we do have those power shut offs.
quote.context_excerpt: so I hope you're in coordination with that because you know especially comes to mind the public safety power shut offs and you know I'm I'm going to sound like a broken record council but I think you know having a more sustainable local source of energy uh to provide our residents in those times of the shut off whether it's a micro grid system, you know, solar gardens that we can tap into in battery system. There's many things we can be looking at to help have a more robust, resilient community when we do have those power shut offs.

workshop_topics.feeds_into: Jefferson County Hazard Mitigation Plan annex expected to return to council in fall 2026 for formal adoption; evacuation policy tools and PSPS protocols may surface in a future business meeting

workshop_topics.title: 2027 Water, Wastewater, and Stormwater Rates and Fees
workshop_topics.tagline: Staff launches annual workshop series with foundational briefing on infrastructure needs and affordability
workshop_topics.lede: Utility staff opened the annual rate-setting workshop series with a foundational overview covering how enterprise funds work, what customers currently pay, the city's 10-year capital planning framework, and the bond-funded infrastructure roadmap adopted in 2022. Staff also presented updates to affordability and hardship credit programs, announced a new autopay option that attracted over 1,000 sign-ups in its first week, and proposed standing up an ad hoc community advisory group to help guide rate and infrastructure decisions through 2030. Council members raised questions about tiered pricing fairness, water conservation incentives, tree canopy protection, and how to ensure capital project criticality ratings are applied rigorously.

options.label: Establish ad hoc community advisory group through 2030
options.summary: Create a short-term, specialized community advisory group — not a formal board or commission — to provide public input on utility infrastructure trade-offs, financial strategies, and affordability through the current period of historic infrastructure reinvestment ending around 2030.

options.label: Refine capital project criticality color-coding system
options.summary: Ensure that red, orange, and yellow project ratings in the 10-year capital planning document reflect true criticality rather than relative queue position, and break large program-level line items into distinct segments so council can make more granular funding decisions.

options.label: Explore drought-aware and family-size-sensitive tiered rate adjustments
options.summary: Investigate whether tiered water rates can be adjusted to account for household size, vegetable gardens or productive water uses, and drought conditions, so that families with more residents or beneficial water uses are not disproportionately penalized.

options.label: Prioritize nonfunctional turf reduction as core drought and conservation policy
options.summary: Focus water conservation policy on eliminating irrigation of nonfunctional turf — grass that serves no active use — rather than applying uniform restrictions, drawing on the Las Vegas model where population grew while total water use fell substantially.

quote.speaker: Randy Moorman
quote.text: I I first just want to commend you on the um the community advisory group. I think that's a fantastic idea and I'm I'm excited to see that get going. Um happy to help get the word out.
quote.context_excerpt: uh thank you. I I first just want to commend you on the um the community advisory group. I think that's a fantastic idea and I'm I'm excited to see that get going. Um happy to help get the word out. So, is there on our website now a place to apply? Is that correct?

quote.speaker: Bob Fifer
quote.text: I would like to see the red not just be the next critical item from going up. Red should be truly critical, high critical, not just go from orange to red just because all the reds are getting addressed.
quote.context_excerpt: Can we just make sure because I I felt like last time we were just moving things into criticality levels. I would like to see the red not just be the next critical item from going up. Red should be truly critical, high critical, not just go from orange to red just because all the reds are getting addressed. Does that make sense?

quote.speaker: Shawna Ambrose
quote.text: nonfunctional turf like we really have to accept we live in a desert. We have to let that go and this drought should be the conversation point to to hold on to that and have smart policy and it can really be done.
quote.context_excerpt: but nonfunctional turf like we really have to accept we live in a desert. We have to let that go and this drought should be the conversation point to to hold on to that and have smart policy and it can really be done. Um I've been saying repeatedly about how um Las Vegas as a city uh increased their population from 2002 to 2020 by 110% and overall the city water usage decreased by 30 40%.

quote.speaker: Lauren Simpson
quote.text: I love the idea of the committee with the infrastructure committee particularly with the timeline. It reminds me very much of the capital improvement committee we did some time ago to help us sort of triangulate what the major projects were.
quote.context_excerpt: I love the capital. I love the idea of the committee with the infrastructure committee particularly with the timeline. It reminds me very much of the capital improvement committee we did some time ago to help us sort of triangulate what the major projects were. Um it feels very sort of a a natural sort of successor to what we did uh some some time ago. So I think that that's really good.

workshop_topics.feeds_into: July 28, 2026 utility workshop; August 25, 2026 follow-up workshop; October 2026 business meeting for first and second reading of 2027 rates and fees adoption

meeting_summary: The Arvada City Council held two workshops on May 12, 2026. In the first, city emergency management staff and Arvada Fire presented wildfire preparedness efforts including the Community Wildfire Protection Plan, evacuation strategies, public safety power shut-off responses, and mitigation projects. In the second, utility staff briefed council on the foundational framework for upcoming 2027 water, wastewater, and stormwater rate-setting decisions, including infrastructure planning, affordability programs, and a proposed community advisory group.

alerts.title: Free Drop-Off Site Open at Long Lake
alerts.body: The City of Arvada is hosting a free tree limb drop-off starting Saturday, May 9 at Long Lake Regional Park.
